title: Client Exports
content:
Client Exports
All client-side components, hooks, and utilities are available from the  import path. These are  modules and can be used in both client components and server components that need to render interactive UI.
Components
A client-side navigation link that renders a standard  element but intercepts clicks for SPA-style navigation. Uses the router context to trigger RSC-powered transitions without a full page reload.
 Type                    Description                                                                                      |
    |
                 The target URL path to navigate to (e.g., , ).                            |
              The content to render inside the link.                                                           |
   All standard  element attributes (, , , etc.) are passed through. |
The  component allows default browser behavior for modifier-key clicks (, , , ) and non-primary mouse buttons, so "open in new tab" works as expected.
Usage
Renders the matched child route segment inside a layout component. Each  reads the current segment depth from context and looks up the next-level child segment in the segment map.
Takes no props. Returns  if no child segment matches.
Usage
Outlets can be nested. Each layout in the route tree renders its own , and the framework automatically resolves which child segment to display based on the current URL and segment key hierarchy.
The top-level provider that wraps the entire application with routing context. This component is used internally by the client entry and is generally not needed in application code. It manages URL state, segment state, navigation transitions, and browser history.
 Type                         Description                                                                                           |
    |
                   The application tree, typically the root segment wrapped in .            |
                      The initial URL from the RSC payload.

title: Route Config
content:
Route Config
Flight Router uses a code-based route configuration. Routes are defined as an array of  objects exported from your routes file (typically ). All types are available from the  import path.
Defines a single route in the routing tree. Routes can be nested to create layouts with child pages.
Properties
 Type                          Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
    |
                       Unique route identifier. Used to build hierarchical segment keys (e.g., , ). Must be unique across all routes.                                                                                                               |
                       URL path segment to match. Supports static segments (), dynamic params (), multi-segment patterns (), and catch-all params (). Omit for layout routes that wrap children without adding to the URL. |
                      When , this route matches only when the parent route's path is matched exactly (no additional path segments). Index routes cannot have children.                                                                                       |
   A function that lazily imports the route module. This enables code splitting -- each route's component is loaded on demand.                                                                                                                  |
                Nested child routes. The parent route's component acts as a layout and must render an  for children to appear.                                                                                                                   |
Example
Path matching rules
- Static segments:  matches  exactly.
- Dynamic params:  matches any single segment and captures it as a parameter. For example,  on path  produces .
- Multi-segment patterns:

title: Server Exports
content:
Server Exports
Flight Router provides server-side APIs for both production and development. The production server is built on Hono, while the development experience is powered by a Vite plugin.
Import: 
Creates a production Hono application that serves your Flight Router app with full SSR, RSC streaming, server actions, and static asset serving.
Options
 Type      Description                                                                                                                                                                                       |
    |
   Path to the build output directory. This is the directory produced by  (defaults to ). Can be relative or absolute -- it is resolved to an absolute path internally. |
What it sets up
The returned Hono app includes the following route handlers:
 Method 
  
 GET    
 GET    
 POST   
 GET    
Usage
Create a  file in your project root:
Then run the production server after building:
How SSR works
On each initial page load,  performs the following:
1. Renders the RSC payload for the requested URL using routes and the client manifest.
2. Buffers the RSC stream and scans for client module references to build a per-page module map.
3. Deserializes the RSC payload into a React tree using .
4. Wraps the tree in  and  (SSR-built versions).
5. Renders the React tree to an HTML stream using .
6. Inlines the RSC payload as  tags after the HTML for client hydration.
The client receives fully rendered HTML on the initial load and hydrates using , then switches to RSC-powered client-side navigation for subsequent page transitions.
Additional server exports
The  module also re-exports lower-level utilities for advanced use cases:
 Description                                                                                                                                   |
  |
 Loads all build manifests (RSC client manifest, SSR manifest, server actions manifest, client entry URL, CSS files) from the build directory. |

title: Build Pipeline
headings: Why bundle React into the RSC runtime?
Why three separate builds for components?
Why scan for CSS imports separately?
content:
Build Pipeline
Flight Router uses a 5-phase production build pipeline powered by Vite and Rollup. Each phase produces artifacts that the next phase depends on, resulting in a fully optimized production bundle.
Run the build with:
Overview
Phase 1: RSC Build
Purpose: Bundle server components with the  condition.
This phase uses Rollup to bundle all route modules and the RSC runtime into server-side JavaScript. The key aspects of this phase:
-  condition: React's server variant is used, which provides  required by . This variant of React excludes client-only APIs like .
-  processing: When the bundler encounters a  directive, it replaces the module contents with client component references (proxies) instead of the actual component code. These references contain a module ID and export name that the client can resolve later.
- RSC runtime bundling: Because the  condition cannot be used globally (SSR needs , etc.), React and  are bundled directly into  with the server variant resolved via custom Rollup plugins.
Output: , 
Phase 2: Client Build
Purpose: Bundle client components, the client entry point, and CSS for the browser.
This phase produces the JavaScript and CSS that runs in the user's browser:
- Client components: Every module marked with  is bundled as a separate entry point, enabling per-route code-splitting. Chunks are loaded on demand when a route is navigated to.
- Client entry: The main entry point that bootstraps the RSC client, sets up the router, and handles hydration.
- CSS entries: Server components can import CSS files (e.g., Tailwind), but those imports do not produce client CSS on their own. The build orchestrator scans for  imports in server components and adds them as explicit client build entries.
- App plugins: The orchestrator loads the application's  to extract user plugins (such as ), filtering out framework-internal plugins, and applies them to this phase.
Output: , , 
Phase 3: SSR Build
Purpose: Bundle client components for server-side rende

title: How It Works
content:
How It Works
Flight Router is built on React Server Components (RSC) and uses React's Flight protocol to stream rendered UI from the server to the client. This page explains the high-level architecture and the request flow for both initial page loads and client-side navigation.
Core Concepts
1. Route Matching
When a request arrives, Flight Router matches the URL against the route tree defined in your application. Routes are organized hierarchically, and each level of nesting can extract dynamic parameters from the URL path.
For example, given the URL , the router walks the route tree:
-  matches the root layout
-  matches the posts layout
-  matches the post detail route, extracting 
Each matched route contributes a segment to the final render.
2. Segment Rendering
Each matched route renders its component into a segment map -- a flat key-value structure where keys represent the nesting path and values are the rendered React trees.
For the URL , the segment map might look like:
Segments are named by concatenating the parent key with the child route name, separated by . This flat structure makes it straightforward to identify which segments changed during navigation.
3. RSC Streaming
Once the segment map is built, it is serialized using React's Flight protocol () and streamed to the client as an RSC payload. Streaming means the client can begin processing the response before the server has finished rendering all segments.
The server renders components in a single pass, and any async data fetching within server components is resolved as part of the stream.
4. Client Hydration
On the client, the RSC stream is deserialized using . The deserialized payload produces a React tree that the client mounts into the DOM.
- On the initial page load, the server also performs SSR to produce HTML. The client then hydrates the pre-rendered HTML using , making it interactive without a full re-render.
- On subsequent navigations, the client receives only the RSC payload (no HTML) and u

title: Segment Diffing
headings: Step 4: Only Changed Segments Are Rendered
Step 5: Response Includes All Segment Keys
Dynamic Params Change at the Same Level
content:
Segment Diffing
Segment diffing is Flight Router's mechanism for minimizing the amount of work done during client-side navigation. When navigating between routes that share layouts, only the segments that actually changed are re-rendered on the server and streamed to the client.
Why Segment Diffing Matters
In a nested routing architecture, navigating from one page to another often shares most of the route tree. For example, navigating from  to  only changes the innermost segment -- the root layout and posts layout remain the same.
Without segment diffing, every navigation would re-render the entire page from the root down. With segment diffing:
- Shared layouts maintain their React state (e.g., form inputs, scroll position, open modals)
- Less data is transferred over the network
- Navigation is faster because only the changed portion of the UI is rendered
Example
Navigating from  to :
Only  is rendered and streamed. The  and  segments are kept as-is on the client.
Navigating from  to :
Here, the root layout is preserved, but the entire posts subtree is replaced by the users subtree.
How It Works
The diffing process involves coordination between the client and server across six steps:
Step 1: Client Sends Previous URL
When the user navigates via a  component, the client includes the current URL in the request headers:
The  header signals that this is an RSC payload request (not a full page load). The  header tells the server where the user is navigating from.
Step 2: Server Matches Both URLs
The server matches both the previous URL () and the new URL () against the route tree. This produces two lists of matched route segments:
Step 3: Server Computes the Diff
The  function compares the two segment lists to determine which segments changed:
- Segments present in both lists with the same route and params are unchanged
- Segments present only in the new list are added
- Segments present only in the previous list are removed (the client will drop them)
- Segments presen

title: Your First Route
content:
Your First Route
This guide walks you through creating the minimum set of files needed to see a working Flight Router application: a root layout, a home page component, and the route configuration that ties them together.
1. Create the root layout
The root layout is the outermost component in your route hierarchy. It renders the full HTML document and uses  to display whichever child route matches the current URL.
Create :
Key points:
-  provides client-side navigation. When the user clicks a , Flight Router fetches only the changed segments from the server instead of doing a full page reload.
-  renders the matched child route. Without it, nested routes would have nowhere to appear.
- The root layout is a server component -- it runs on the server and its output is streamed to the client. There is no need for a  directive.
2. Create a home page
Route components are server components by default. They can be  functions, which means you can  data fetches directly in the component body without needing  or a data-loading library.
Create :
Because this is a server component, the timestamp is computed on the server at request time. Each navigation to this page produces a fresh timestamp without any client-side JavaScript.
3. Define the route configuration
The route configuration maps URL paths to components. It is a plain TypeScript file that exports an array of  objects.
Create :
Here is what each field does:
-  -- A unique identifier for the route. Flight Router uses this internally for segment diffing and partial updates.
-  -- The URL segment this route matches. The root uses an empty string  because it wraps all other routes.
-  -- Marks this as an index route. It matches when the parent path is visited exactly (in this case, ).
-  -- A function that returns a dynamic . Flight Router uses this for code splitting so that only the components needed for the current page are loaded.
Note that the import paths use  extensions (e.g., ). This follows the TypeScript/ESM conve

title: Installation
content:
Installation
This guide walks you through installing Flight Router and its required dependencies.
Prerequisites
Before you begin, make sure you have the following installed:
- Node.js 20+ -- Flight Router requires Node.js version 20 or later. You can check your version with .
- A package manager -- npm (included with Node.js), pnpm, or yarn.
Install dependencies
Flight Router builds on React 19, React Server Components, Vite, and Hono for the production server. Install the core dependencies:
Then install the required dev dependencies for building and running the development server:
If you use pnpm or yarn, substitute the appropriate command:
Optional: Tailwind CSS
Flight Router has built-in support for Tailwind CSS v4. To add it to your project:
Then create a CSS file (for example, ) and import Tailwind:
Import this file in your root layout component so that styles are available throughout your application. See Vite Configuration for how to add the Tailwind plugin to your Vite config.
TypeScript configuration
Create a  in the root of your project:
Verify your setup
Your  should look similar to this:
Note the  field -- Flight Router uses ES modules throughout.
Next steps
Now that you have everything installed, set up your project structure and Vite configuration, then create your first route.

title: Project Structure
content:
Project Structure
Flight Router uses a straightforward project layout. Routes are defined explicitly in a configuration file rather than derived from the filesystem, giving you full control over your routing hierarchy.
Typical layout
File reference
The Vite configuration file where you register the Flight Router plugin and any other Vite plugins (such as Tailwind CSS). This file tells Flight Router where to find your route definitions.
See Vite Configuration for the full details.
The central route configuration file. It exports an array of  objects that define the URL structure of your application, including nested layouts and dynamic segments.
Each route has:
-  -- A unique string identifier for the route.
-  (optional) -- The URL path segment this route matches (e.g., , ). Omit for index routes.
-  (optional) -- When , this route matches when the parent path is an exact match.
-  -- A function returning a dynamic  of the route module.
-  (optional) -- Nested child routes rendered inside the parent's .
The root layout component. This is the outermost component in your route hierarchy and is responsible for rendering the , , and  tags. It uses the  component to render matched child routes.
Global CSS for your application. If you are using Tailwind CSS, this file typically contains:
Import this file in your root layout () so the styles are included in the build:
A conventional directory for route components. You can organize files however you like -- Flight Router does not enforce filesystem conventions. The route configuration in  determines which files map to which URLs.
Server components
Route components are React Server Components by default. They can be  functions and can use  to fetch data directly:
Client components
Files that need browser interactivity (state, effects, event handlers) must include the  directive at the top:
Client components can be imported and rendered inside server components.
Nested layouts
A layout component renders  to display its child

title: Vite Configuration
content:
Vite Configuration
Flight Router integrates with Vite through a dedicated plugin that handles React Server Component rendering, server-side rendering, hot module replacement, and the production build pipeline.
Basic configuration
Create a  file in the root of your project:
The two required plugins are:
1.  -- Enables React Fast Refresh and JSX transformation during development.
2.  -- The Flight Router plugin that provides RSC support, SSR, client/server component splitting, and the build orchestrator.
Plugin options
The  plugin accepts the following options:
 Type      Description                                                         |
    |
   Path to the route configuration file, relative to the project root. |
Adding Tailwind CSS
To use Tailwind CSS v4 with Flight Router, install the dependencies and add the Tailwind plugin to your Vite config. The Tailwind plugin should come before the other plugins:
Then create a CSS file that imports Tailwind (for example, ):
Import it in your root layout so the styles are included throughout your application:
Running the development server
Start the development server with:
This starts a dev server (typically at ) with:
- Server-side rendering (SSR) -- Pages are fully rendered to HTML on the server for fast initial loads.
- Hot Module Replacement (HMR) -- Changes to both server and client components are reflected instantly without a full page reload.
- React Server Components -- Server components run on the server and stream their output to the client. Client components are hydrated in the browser.
Production build
To build your application for production, run:
This executes a multi-phase build that produces:
1. RSC bundle -- Server components bundled with the  condition.
2. Client bundle -- Client components and entry point optimized for the browser.
3. SSR bundle -- Client components compiled for server-side rendering.
4. Manifests -- Module maps that connect server and client components.
5. Server entry -- Your  bundled

title: Client Components
headings: Importing client components from server components
When to use client vs server components
content:
Client Components
By default, all components in Flight Router are React Server Components -- they run on the server, have no bundle cost, and can use / for data fetching. When you need interactivity (state, effects, event handlers, browser APIs), mark a file with the  directive to make it a client component.
The "use client" directive
Add  as the very first line of a file to mark all exports in that file as client components:
The directive applies to the entire file. Every component exported from a  file is a client component.
Naming convention
While not strictly required, using the  suffix for client component files makes it easy to identify them at a glance:
This convention is recommended but optional -- the  directive is what actually determines the component type.
Importing client components from server components
Server components can import and render client components. Props passed from server to client components are serialized across the server-client boundary:
The static parts of the page (heading, paragraph, timestamp) are rendered on the server. The  component is sent to the client as a reference, and the client loads and renders it with full interactivity.
Using React hooks
Client components have access to all React hooks:
Forms with controlled inputs
Client components are the right place for interactive forms with controlled inputs and client-side validation:
Passing props across the boundary
Props from server to client components must be serializable. You can pass:
- Strings, numbers, booleans, null, undefined
- Plain objects and arrays (containing serializable values)
- Dates
- Server Action functions (see the Server Actions guide)
- React elements (JSX)
You cannot pass:
- Functions (except Server Actions)
- Class instances
- Symbols
- DOM nodes
SSR behavior
Client components are rendered during server-side rendering (SSR) in addition to running on the client. This means:
- The initial HTML includes the rendered output of client components.
- The pag

title: CSS & Styling
content:
CSS & Styling
Flight Router supports several approaches to styling your application, including Tailwind CSS v4, CSS modules, and global CSS files. All styles work in both server and client components, and are automatically extracted and optimized in production builds.
Tailwind CSS v4
Tailwind CSS v4 is the recommended styling approach. It integrates directly with Vite through the  plugin.
Installation
Install the required packages:
Vite Configuration
Add the Tailwind plugin to your :
Create Your Stylesheet
Create a CSS file that imports Tailwind:
Import in Your Root Layout
Import the stylesheet in your root layout so it applies globally:
Using Tailwind Classes
Use Tailwind utility classes in both server and client components:
Global CSS
Import any CSS file in a server component to make it globally available:
CSS Modules
CSS modules scope styles to individual components, preventing class name collisions. Use the  extension.
Import the module and use the  object to reference class names:
CSS modules work in both server and client components.
Combining Approaches
You can freely mix styling approaches within the same application. For example, use Tailwind for utility styles and CSS modules for complex component-specific styles:
Production Builds
In production, Flight Router's build pipeline automatically:
- Extracts CSS into separate optimized files
- Minifies and deduplicates styles
- Generates hashed filenames for cache busting
- Injects  tags into the server-rendered HTML
No additional configuration is required. Run  and all CSS is handled for you.

title: Data Fetching
headings: Fetching data in a route component
content:
Data Fetching
Flight Router route components are React Server Components. This means you can use / directly in your components to fetch data on the server -- no , , or third-party data fetching libraries required.
Fetching data in a route component
Any route component can be an  function. Use standard  calls (or any async operation) directly in the component body:
The data is fetched entirely on the server. The client never sees the fetch call or the raw API response -- it only receives the rendered HTML and RSC payload.
Using route params
Dynamic route segments are passed to your component as a  prop with the type . Use them to fetch data for a specific resource:
When the URL is , the component receives . The param names correspond to the dynamic segments defined in your route configuration (e.g.,  becomes ).
Parallel data fetching
When you need to fetch multiple resources, use  to run requests in parallel and avoid waterfalls:
Both the post and its comments are fetched at the same time, cutting the total wait time roughly in half compared to sequential requests.
Streaming
Flight Router streams the RSC response to the client as each part of the component tree resolves. This means:
- No waterfall: Nested layouts and their children can fetch data independently. Each segment renders as soon as its data is ready.
- Fast first paint: The shell of the page (layouts, static content) appears immediately while async content is still loading.
- No loading spinners required: In many cases, data resolves quickly enough on the server that the user sees the complete page. For slower requests, you can use React's  to show a fallback.
The layout renders immediately with the "Loading posts..." fallback, and the posts list streams in once the fetch completes.
What you do not need
Because route components run on the server, you can drop the client-side data fetching patterns you may be used to:
 Still needed?                       |
  |
 No                                  |
 No

title: Layouts & Outlets
content:
Layouts & Outlets
Layouts are route components that wrap child routes with shared UI -- things like navigation bars, sidebars, headers, and footers. The  component renders whichever child route matches the current URL.
The Outlet component
Import  from  and render it wherever you want the child route to appear:
 automatically resolves the correct child segment from the current route match. If no child matches, it renders nothing.
Root layout
The root layout is the outermost route in your configuration. It typically renders the , , and  elements, along with site-wide navigation:
In the route configuration, the root layout uses  so it matches every URL:
Nested layouts
Any route with  in the route configuration acts as a layout. The component for that route should render an  to display its children.
For example, a posts section with its own layout:
With the corresponding route configuration:
When the user navigates to , the posts layout renders with the index page inside its . When they navigate to , the layout stays mounted and only the outlet content changes to the detail page.
Layout preservation during navigation
Flight Router uses segment-level diffing to minimize re-renders during client-side navigation. When the user navigates between sibling routes, only the changed segments are re-rendered on the server and sent to the client. Parent layouts remain untouched.
For example, navigating from  to :
1. The root layout () is unchanged -- it is preserved.
2. The posts layout () is unchanged -- it is preserved.
3. Only the child segment changes from  to .
This means:
- Layout state (like scroll position, form inputs, or client component state in the layout) is preserved.
- Only the minimal amount of data is transferred from the server.
- The navigation feels instant because most of the page stays in place.
Multiple levels of nesting
You can nest layouts as deeply as you need. Each level adds another :
The dashboard layout can include its own navigation, and both the ro

title: Navigation & Links
content:
Navigation & Links
Flight Router provides a  component and several hooks for navigating between pages in your application. Client-side navigation fetches only the changed segments of the page, avoiding full page reloads.
The Link Component
Use the  component from  for declarative navigation. It renders a standard  element and intercepts clicks to perform SPA-style navigation.
Since  renders a regular  tag, it supports progressive enhancement. If JavaScript has not loaded, the link works as a normal browser navigation.
Programmatic Navigation
Use the  hook when you need to navigate in response to events other than link clicks.
The  hook returns an object with the following properties:
 Type                        
  
                     
      
  
        
     
Loading Indicators
Use the  hook to show loading indicators during page transitions.
The  hook returns:
 Type                  
  
  
Reading the Current Location
Use the  hook to read the current pathname.
Active Link Styling
Combine  with  to highlight the currently active link.
Reading Route Parameters
Use the  hook to access dynamic route parameters. For a route defined as , the  parameter is available through this hook.
The  hook returns a  containing all matched dynamic segments from the current URL.
Segment Diffing
When navigating between pages, Flight Router only fetches and re-renders the segments of the page that have changed. For example, navigating from  to  will re-render the post content but keep the shared layout intact. This makes navigations fast and efficient, since unchanged parts of the page are preserved without any extra work on your part.

title: Routing
content:
Routing
Flight Router uses a centralized route configuration to define your application's URL structure. Routes are defined as a tree of  objects in a single  file, giving you full control over path matching, nesting, and code splitting.
The RouteConfig interface
Import the  type from  and export a  array:
Each  object has the following properties:
 Type

Basic routes
A minimal route tree with a root layout, an index (home) page, and an about page:
- The root route has , which makes it a layout route -- it matches every URL and wraps all child routes.
- The home route has , so it renders when the URL matches the parent path exactly ().
- The about route matches .
Dynamic segments
Prefix a path segment with  to capture a dynamic value. The captured value is available in the route component via the  prop.
When the URL is , the component receives  with the value .
You can include multiple segments in a single path:
This matches  and provides  as .
Catch-all segments
Append  to a dynamic segment to match the rest of the URL path:
For the URL , the component receives  with the value .
Nested routes
Any route with a  array becomes a layout. The parent component renders an  to display whichever child route matches the current URL.
This produces the following URL structure:

The layout component persists across navigations between its children, so shared UI like navigation tabs is not re-rendered.
Full example

Code splitting
Each route's  property is a function that returns a dynamic . This means route modules are only loaded when they are needed, and Vite automatically code-splits them into separate chunks f

title: Server Actions
content:
Server Actions
Server actions let you define functions that run on the server and can be called directly from client components. They are the primary way to handle form submissions, data mutations, and other server-side operations in Flight Router.
Defining Server Actions
Add the  directive at the top of a file to mark all exported functions as server actions.
Server action files can export multiple functions. Each exported function becomes a callable server action.
Using Server Actions with Forms
The recommended way to use server actions is with React's  hook. This gives you the previous state, a form action dispatcher, and a pending flag.
The  hook accepts three arguments:
1. The server action function -- imported from a  file.
2. The initial state -- the starting value before any action has been dispatched.
3. An optional permalink -- a URL used for progressive enhancement.
It returns a tuple of:
1. Current state -- the latest value returned by the action.
2. Form action -- a dispatch function to pass as the form's  prop.
3. isPending -- a boolean indicating whether the action is currently executing.
Server Action Signatures
Server actions used with  receive two arguments:
The return value becomes the new state that  provides to your component.
Progressive Enhancement
Server actions support progressive enhancement out of the box. When JavaScript is not available or has not yet loaded, forms using server actions will still submit and work correctly through standard HTML form submission. This means your forms are functional before hydration completes.
No extra configuration is needed. Flight Router automatically handles the form submission on the server and returns the updated state to the client.
Inline Server Actions
You can also define server actions inline within server components by adding  at the top of the function body.
Error Handling
Handle errors within your server action and return them as part of the state.
Then display the error in your client componen

title: SSR
content:
Server-Side Rendering (SSR)
Flight Router includes built-in server-side rendering. On initial page load, the server renders your application to full HTML so the browser can display content immediately. The client then hydrates the HTML to make it interactive. No configuration is required.
How It Works
When a user requests a page, Flight Router performs these steps:
1. RSC rendering -- The server renders your React Server Components into an RSC payload (a serialized representation of the component tree).
2. HTML generation -- The RSC payload is deserialized and rendered to an HTML stream using .
3. RSC payload inlining -- The RSC payload is embedded in the HTML as  tags so the client does not need a separate fetch to begin hydration.
4. Streaming to the client -- The HTML is streamed to the browser for fast First Contentful Paint (FCP).
5. Client hydration -- The client-side React picks up the inlined RSC payload and hydrates the HTML, attaching event handlers and enabling interactivity.
This architecture eliminates the client-server waterfall that traditional SSR approaches suffer from. The RSC payload is already in the HTML, so hydration can begin immediately without waiting for additional network requests.
Production Builds
The  command generates everything needed for SSR automatically:
The build pipeline produces:
- RSC bundle -- Server components bundled with the  condition.
- Client bundle -- Client components optimized for the browser.
- SSR bundle -- Client components bundled for server-side rendering.
- Manifests -- Mapping files that connect RSC references to client and SSR chunks.
- Server entry -- The production server that handles requests and performs SSR.
Start the production server:
Every page request is server-rendered with the full HTML content.
Dev Mode SSR
SSR also works in development mode. When you run the dev server, initial page loads are server-rendered just like in production. This means you can develop and debug SSR behavior without running
